Expert knowledge integration in species distribution model (SDM) - A Bayesian approach

Camila Diaz

2026-06-04

Overview

This document implements a two-step Bayesian species distribution model (SDM) for monarch butterfly (Danaus plexippus) in Canada.

Step 1 — Expert-knowledge model (Stan). Expert-elicited range limits and point observations are used to define an expert-informed prior and a small occurrence dataset. Three Stan variants are compared: a baseline logistic model, a misclassification model that treats low-confidence labels as imperfect, and a weighted-likelihood model that down-weights low-confidence points. The posterior distributions of the regression coefficients are then extracted.

Step 2 — Model update with occurrence data (INLA). The Stan posteriors are propagated as Gaussian priors into an R-INLA binomial model that includes a spatial random effect (SPDE/Matérn). The workflow is also run with non-informative priors to provide a baseline comparison.

1. Setup

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(sf)
library(INLA)
library(geodata)
library(terra)

sf handles all vector spatial operations (read/transform/intersect); terra handles raster operations; INLA fits the spatial Bayesian model in Step 2; dplyr/tidyr handle tabular data wrangling; ggplot2 produces maps.

2. Data loading


epsg = 3857 
epsg_crs = "EPSG:3857"

# Study area
study_area0 <- st_read("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/data/study_area/canada.shp",
  quiet = TRUE)

study_area <- study_area0 |>
  st_transform(epsg) |>
  st_union()|>
  st_simplify(dTolerance = 1000)|> # 1 km tolerance in EPSG:3857
  st_make_valid()

# Occurrence data
monarch_data <- st_read("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/data/species/PA_monarch_CAN.shp",
  quiet = TRUE) #en 

monarch_sf <- monarch_data |>
  st_transform(crs = epsg)

# Expert knowledge data
ek_pnt <- read.csv("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/data/expert_knowledge/Points.csv")
ek_answrs <- read.csv("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/data/expert_knowledge/Responses.csv")

# Environmental layers - Ouranos
tas_mean0 <- rast("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/data/predictors/tas_mean.tif")
pr_mean0 <- rast("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/data/predictors/pr_mean.tif")

Four data sources are loaded:

Object Contents
study_area Canada polygon, dissolved and simplified to 1 km, in EPSG:3857 (Web Mercator, units = metres)
monarch_sf Presence/absence occurrence records, reprojected to EPSG:3857
ek_pnt Expert-elicited point locations with label (presence/avoid) and confidence level
ek_answrs Expert-elicited range limits for temperature and precipitation (one row per survey response)
tas_mean0, pr_mean0 Mean annual temperature and precipitation rasters (Ouranos climate data)

EPSG:3857 is used throughout so that all distance-based operations (mesh, simplification) are in metres.

3. Environmental layer preparation

tas_mean <- project(tas_mean0, epsg_crs)
tas_mask <- mask(tas_mean, vect(study_area))
mt <- global(tas_mask, mean, na.rm = TRUE)[1,1]
st <- global(tas_mask, sd, na.rm = TRUE)[1,1]
std_tas <- (tas_mask - mt) / st


pr_mean <- project(pr_mean0, epsg_crs)
pr_mask <- mask(pr_mean, vect(study_area))
mp <- global(pr_mask, mean, na.rm = TRUE)[1,1]
sp <- global(pr_mask, sd, na.rm = TRUE)[1,1]
std_pr <- (pr_mask - mp) / sp


env <- c(std_tas, std_pr)
names(env) <- c("temp", "prec")

env
#> class       : SpatRaster 
#> dimensions  : 684, 1499, 2  (nrow, ncol, nlyr)
#> resolution  : 26720.37, 26738.21  (x, y)
#> extent      : -20037506, 20016329, 968774.1, 19257708  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax)
#> coord. ref. : WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator (EPSG:3857) 
#> source(s)   : memory
#> names       :      temp,     prec 
#> min values  : -2.487619, -1.91499 
#> max values  :  1.973588, 19.33590

env_df <- as.data.frame(env, xy = TRUE, na.rm = FALSE)

# Track which cells are non-NA
valid_idx <- complete.cases(env_df[, c("temp", "prec")])
env_valid <- env_df[valid_idx, ]

Each raster is reprojected to EPSG:3857 and clipped to the study area before standardising. Standardisation centres and scales each layer to zero mean and unit standard deviation across all non-NA cells within the study area:

\[z_i = \frac{x_i - \bar{x}}{s_x}\]

where \(\bar{x}\) and \(s_x\) are the study-area-wide mean and standard deviation. This places both covariates on a common scale so that the expert-elicited range limits (which are expressed in the original units) can be expressed in the same standardised units before being passed to Stan. The vectors mt, st, mp, sp are saved and reused in the expert-knowledge chunk to standardise the expert responses with the same transformation.

valid_idx flags the raster cells that have non-missing values for both covariates; it is reused later to populate prediction rasters from a flat vector of predictions.

4. Expert knowledge processing


# Spatialize expert points
sf_pnt <- ek_pnt |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("long", "lat"),
           crs = 4326) |>
  st_intersection(study_area0) |>
  st_transform(crs = epsg)

head(sf_pnt, 5)
#> Simple feature collection with 5 features and 14 fields
#> Geometry type: POINT
#> Dimension:     XY
#> Bounding box:  xmin: -8644652 ymin: 5836324 xmax: -7842596 ymax: 6384225
#> Projected CRS: WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator
#>               survey_id                    time point_id     type confidence
#> 1 20260427210652_0c21a5 2026-04-27 18:44:45 UTC        1 presence       High
#> 2 20260427210652_0c21a5 2026-04-27 18:44:45 UTC        2 presence       High
#> 3 20260427210652_0c21a5 2026-04-27 18:44:45 UTC        3 presence     Medium
#> 4 20260427210652_0c21a5 2026-04-27 18:44:45 UTC        4 presence        Low
#> 5 20260427210652_0c21a5 2026-04-27 18:44:45 UTC        5 presence        Low
#>   notes iso3       status colr_cd   name  contnnt           region i_3166_
#> 1        CAN Member State     CAN Canada Americas Northern America      CA
#> 2        CAN Member State     CAN Canada Americas Northern America      CA
#> 3        CAN Member State     CAN Canada Americas Northern America      CA
#> 4        CAN Member State     CAN Canada Americas Northern America      CA
#> 5        CAN Member State     CAN Canada Americas Northern America      CA
#>   frnch_s                 geometry
#> 1  Canada POINT (-8292530 5836324)
#> 2  Canada POINT (-8644652 5934163)
#> 3  Canada POINT (-7842596 6080922)
#> 4  Canada POINT (-8449029 6257033)
#> 5  Canada POINT (-8077344 6384225)

sf_pnt <- sf_pnt |>
  mutate(y = as.integer(type == "presence"),
         temp = terra::extract(env$temp, vect(sf_pnt))[,2],
         prec = terra::extract(env$prec, vect(sf_pnt))[,2])

# --- Map point-level confidence to model quantities ---
# Weighted likelihood option: w = fractional weight of each observation.
#   High confidence -> full weight; Low -> quarter weight.
conf_weights   <- c(Low = 0.25, Medium = 0.50, High = 1.00)

sf_pnt <- sf_pnt |>
  mutate(
    w    = conf_weights[confidence]
  )

m <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = study_area, fill = "grey83", color = "black") +
  geom_sf(data = sf_pnt, aes(colour = type), alpha = 0.4, size = 2, shape = 16) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    name   = NULL,
    values = c("avoid"  = "red",
               "presence" = "green"),
    guide  = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 3, alpha = 1))
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    legend.position   = "bottom",
    axis.text         = element_blank(),
    panel.grid        = element_blank()
  )
m

#ggsave(paste0("C:/Users/maria/Documents/Chapter1/bayesian_model/results/data_surveys.png"), plot = m, width = 8, height = 8, dpi = 300)

# standarize range values and Keep the necessary columns
answrs_select <- ek_answrs |>
  mutate(std_min_temp = (min.temp - mt) / st,
         std_max_temp = (max.temp - mt) / st,
         std_min_prec = (min.prec - mp) / sp,
         std_max_prec = (max.prec - mp) / sp) |>
  select("survey_id", "std_min_temp", "std_max_temp", "confidence.temp", "std_min_prec", "std_max_prec", "confidence.prec")

This chunk converts the expert data into two model-ready objects.

Expert points (sf_pnt). The raw CSV coordinates are converted to an sf object in WGS 84, clipped to the study area, then reprojected. The binary response is defined as \(y_n = 1\) if the label is "presence" and \(y_n = 0\) otherwise. Standardised temperature and precipitation values are extracted at each point from the env stack.

Confidence → model quantities. Each point carries a self-assessed confidence level (Low, Medium, High). This is mapped to two different model quantities depending on which Stan variant is used:

Confidence sens / spec w
Low 0.60 0.25
Medium 0.80 0.50
High 0.95 1.00

sens and spec feed the misclassification model (Option 1); w feeds the weighted-likelihood model (Option 2).

Expert range responses (answrs_select). The elicited minimum and maximum values for temperature and precipitation are standardised with the same \(\bar{x}\) and \(s_x\) used for the rasters, so they are directly comparable to the standardised covariate values. These become the prior centres \(a_k^*\), \(b_k^*\), \(c_k^*\) for the triangular response function (see §5).

5. Stan data preparation

Using Stan to run step 1, expert informed model

# Cache compiled Stan C++ to disk so subsequent knits skip recompilation
# (~2-3 min saved per model). The .rds files are written next to the .stan files.
library(posterior)
library(loo)
library(rstan)
rstan_options(auto_write = TRUE)

sigma_levels <- c(low = 0.01, medium = 0.5, high = 1.0)
sigma_levels
#>    low medium   high 
#>   0.01   0.50   1.00

# Base data shared by all Stan models
stan_data_base <- list(
  N  = length(sf_pnt$y),
  y  = as.integer(sf_pnt$y),
  x1 = sf_pnt$temp,
  x2 = sf_pnt$prec,

  # Covariate 1 expert values + uncertainty
  a1_star  = answrs_select$std_min_temp,
  b1_star  = answrs_select$std_max_temp,
  c1_star  = (answrs_select$std_max_temp / 2),
  sigma_a1 = sigma_levels["medium"],
  sigma_b1 = sigma_levels["low"],
  sigma_c1 = sigma_levels["medium"],

  # Covariate 2 expert values + uncertainty
  a2_star  = answrs_select$std_min_prec,
  b2_star  = answrs_select$std_max_prec,
  c2_star  = (answrs_select$std_max_prec / 2),
  sigma_a2 = sigma_levels["low"],
  sigma_b2 = sigma_levels["low"],
  sigma_c2 = sigma_levels["low"],

  # Slope priors (both positive effects expected)
  beta1_mu    = 1,
  beta1_sigma = sigma_levels["medium"],
  beta2_mu    = 1,
  beta2_sigma = sigma_levels["medium"]
)

# Original model (no confidence weighting — baseline)
stan_data <- stan_data_base


# weighted likelihood model (weight per point)
stan_data_weighted <- c(
  stan_data_base,
  list(w = sf_pnt$w)
)

Two data lists are assembled, all sharing a common base. The base contains:

  • N, y, x1, x2 — the expert point dataset.
  • Triangular shape priors for each covariate: the expert-elicited lower limit \(a_k^*\), upper limit \(b_k^*\), and implied mode \(c_k^* = b_k^*/2\) (in standardised units), together with their uncertainty levels sigma_*.
  • Slope direction priors (beta1_mu = 1, beta2_mu = 1) encoding the expectation that both temperature and precipitation have a positive association with occurrence probability.

sigma_levels encodes three qualitative uncertainty tiers (low = 0.01, medium = 0.5, high = 1.0). A tighter sigma means the Stan sampler is constrained to stay near the expert-stated value; a wider sigma allows more learning from the data. The minimum temperature bound \(b_1^*\) is assigned sigma_low (tight) because experts are typically more certain about hard upper limits; the lower bound \(a_1^*\) and mode \(c_1^*\) are given sigma_medium.

The two option-specific lists append sens/spec vectors (Option 1) or w weights (Option 2) to the shared base.

6. Stan Model — Baseline

Model specification

All three Stan models share the same core statistical structure. The observed binary response \(y_n \in \{0, 1\}\) at expert point \(n\) is modelled as:

\[y_n \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_n)\]

\[\text{logit}(p_n) = \alpha + \beta_1\, f_1(x_{1n}) + \beta_2\, f_2(x_{2n})\]

where \(f_k(\cdot)\) is a triangular response function that encodes the expert’s belief that the species has a unimodal suitability response within the range \([a_k, b_k]\) with peak at the mode \(c_k\):

\[f_k(x;\, a_k, c_k, b_k) = \begin{cases} 0 & x < a_k \text{ or } x > b_k \\ \dfrac{2(x - a_k)}{(b_k - a_k)(c_k - a_k)} & a_k \leq x \leq c_k \\[6pt] \dfrac{2(b_k - x)}{(b_k - a_k)(b_k - c_k)} & c_k < x \leq b_k \end{cases}\]

The function equals 0 outside \([a_k, b_k]\) and reaches a maximum of 1 at \(x = c_k\).

Ordered parameterisation

Stan requires the constraint \(a_k < c_k < b_k\) to be enforced explicitly. This is done by sampling log-gap parameters and exponentiating:

\[a_k = a_{k,\text{raw}}\] \[c_k = a_k + \exp(\text{log\_gap}_{c_k})\] \[b_k = c_k + \exp(\text{log\_gap}_{b_k})\]

Because \(\exp(\cdot) > 0\), the ordering \(a_k < c_k < b_k\) is guaranteed for all sample values.

Priors

The shape parameters are given Gaussian priors centred on the expert-elicited values (in standardised units):

\[a_{k,\text{raw}} \sim \mathcal{N}(a_k^*, \sigma_{a_k})\] \[\log(c_k - a_k) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log(c_k^* - a_k^*),\; \sigma_{c_k}\right)\] \[\log(b_k - c_k) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\log(b_k^* - c_k^*),\; \sigma_{b_k}\right)\]

The intercept and slope priors encode expected positive effects:

\[\alpha \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\] \[\beta_k \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\beta_k},\, \sigma_{\beta_k})\]

# --- Baseline model (no confidence, original) ---
stan_code <- stan_model(
  file = "Step1-Expert_model.stan",
  verbose = FALSE)

fit <- sampling(stan_code, 
                data = stan_data, 
                chains = 4, 
                refresh = 0,
                show_messages = FALSE, 
                open_progress = FALSE)

print(fit, pars = c("beta1", "beta2"))
#> Inference for Stan model: anon_model.
#> 4 chains, each with iter=2000; warmup=1000; thin=1; 
#> post-warmup draws per chain=1000, total post-warmup draws=4000.
#> 
#>       mean se_mean   sd 2.5%  25%  50%  75% 97.5% n_eff Rhat
#> beta1 2.28    0.01 0.38 1.55 2.03 2.27 2.52  3.06  4637    1
#> beta2 0.99    0.01 0.50 0.01 0.66 0.99 1.33  1.98  4745    1
#> 
#> Samples were drawn using NUTS(diag_e) at Thu Jun  4 16:41:17 2026.
#> For each parameter, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size,
#> and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at 
#> convergence, Rhat=1).

stan_model() compiles the Stan source code to C++. sampling() runs the No-U-Turn Sampler (NUTS), a variant of Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, with 4 independent chains (default 2000 iterations each, 1000 warm-up). NUTS adaptively tunes the step size and trajectory length so no manual tuning is needed.

The generated quantities block in the Stan file computes two diagnostics per draw:

  • y_rep[n] — posterior predictive replicate (for graphical posterior predictive checks).
  • log_lik[n] — observation-level log-likelihood (for LOO-CV model comparison).

7. Stan Model — Option 2: Weighted likelihood

Each point contributes to the log-posterior proportionally to its confidence weight (High=1, Medium=0.5, Low=0.25), treating low-confidence points as fractional observations.

Reference: Isaac et al. (2014), Botella et al. (2020)


stan_code_weighted <- stan_model(file = "Step1-Expert_model_weighted.stan",
  verbose = FALSE)

fit_weighted <- sampling(
  stan_code_weighted,
  data    = stan_data_weighted,
  chains  = 4,
  refresh = 0,
  show_messages = FALSE,
  open_progress = FALSE
)
print(fit_weighted, pars = c("beta1", "beta2"))
#> Inference for Stan model: anon_model.
#> 4 chains, each with iter=2000; warmup=1000; thin=1; 
#> post-warmup draws per chain=1000, total post-warmup draws=4000.
#> 
#>       mean se_mean  sd 2.5%  25%  50%  75% 97.5% n_eff Rhat
#> beta1 2.16    0.01 0.4 1.39 1.89 2.16 2.43  2.93  4053    1
#> beta2 0.99    0.01 0.5 0.00 0.66 0.99 1.30  1.98  4707    1
#> 
#> Samples were drawn using NUTS(diag_e) at Thu Jun  4 16:41:30 2026.
#> For each parameter, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size,
#> and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at 
#> convergence, Rhat=1).

Rather than modelling label error explicitly, the weighted-likelihood model scales each point’s contribution to the log-posterior by a confidence weight \(w_n\):

\[\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{n=1}^N w_n \log p(y_n \mid \boldsymbol{\theta})\]

where \(w_n \in \{0.25, 0.50, 1.00\}\) for Low, Medium, and High confidence respectively. A point with \(w_n = 0.25\) contributes only one-quarter as much to the log-posterior as a high-confidence point, so it has proportionally less influence on the shape of the posterior. This is equivalent to treating it as a fractional observation.

In Stan this is written as target += w[n] * bernoulli_logit_lpmf(y[n] | mu).

LOO note: log_lik in generated quantities uses the unweighted likelihood in all two models so that LOO-CV scores reflect true predictive accuracy on held-out data, not the training-time weighting scheme.

8. HMC Diagnostics

# Baseline
check_hmc_diagnostics(fit)
#> 
#> Divergences:
#> 
#> Tree depth:
#> 
#> Energy:
print(fit, pars = c("alpha", "beta1", "beta2",
                    "a1", "c1", "b1",
                    "a2", "c2", "b2"),
      probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))
#> Inference for Stan model: anon_model.
#> 4 chains, each with iter=2000; warmup=1000; thin=1; 
#> post-warmup draws per chain=1000, total post-warmup draws=4000.
#> 
#>        mean se_mean   sd  2.5%   50% 97.5% n_eff Rhat
#> alpha -1.19    0.01 0.36 -1.92 -1.18 -0.51  4269    1
#> beta1  2.28    0.01 0.38  1.55  2.27  3.06  4637    1
#> beta2  0.99    0.01 0.50  0.01  0.99  1.98  4745    1
#> a1     0.94    0.00 0.13  0.64  0.95  1.14  2690    1
#> c1     1.52    0.00 0.08  1.37  1.51  1.71  3865    1
#> b1     2.53    0.00 0.08  2.38  2.52  2.71  3858    1
#> a2    -1.04    0.00 0.01 -1.06 -1.04 -1.02  5199    1
#> c2     9.63    0.00 0.11  9.42  9.62  9.84  5454    1
#> b2    19.25    0.00 0.14 18.98 19.25 19.54  5892    1
#> 
#> Samples were drawn using NUTS(diag_e) at Thu Jun  4 16:41:17 2026.
#> For each parameter, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size,
#> and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at 
#> convergence, Rhat=1).


# Option 1 — weighted
check_hmc_diagnostics(fit_weighted)
#> 
#> Divergences:
#> 
#> Tree depth:
#> 
#> Energy:
print(fit_weighted, pars = c("alpha", "beta1", "beta2",
                              "a1", "c1", "b1",
                              "a2", "c2", "b2"),
      probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))
#> Inference for Stan model: anon_model.
#> 4 chains, each with iter=2000; warmup=1000; thin=1; 
#> post-warmup draws per chain=1000, total post-warmup draws=4000.
#> 
#>        mean se_mean   sd  2.5%   50% 97.5% n_eff Rhat
#> alpha -1.07    0.01 0.36 -1.81 -1.05 -0.37  3415    1
#> beta1  2.16    0.01 0.40  1.39  2.16  2.93  4053    1
#> beta2  0.99    0.01 0.50  0.00  0.99  1.98  4707    1
#> a1     0.90    0.00 0.17  0.51  0.93  1.15  2436    1
#> c1     1.53    0.00 0.10  1.35  1.52  1.75  4492    1
#> b1     2.54    0.00 0.11  2.36  2.53  2.76  4525    1
#> a2    -1.04    0.00 0.01 -1.06 -1.04 -1.02  4148    1
#> c2     9.62    0.00 0.11  9.41  9.62  9.84  4487    1
#> b2    19.25    0.00 0.14 18.96 19.25 19.54  5114    1
#> 
#> Samples were drawn using NUTS(diag_e) at Thu Jun  4 16:41:30 2026.
#> For each parameter, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size,
#> and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at 
#> convergence, Rhat=1).

check_hmc_diagnostics() reports three NUTS-specific diagnostics that signal problems with the posterior geometry:

Diagnostic Concern if…
Divergent transitions Any divergences indicate the sampler was unable to explore part of the posterior; inferences may be biased.
Max tree depth exceeded The trajectory was truncated, suggesting slow mixing or a difficult geometry.
\(\hat{R}\) (Rhat) Values \(> 1.01\) indicate the chains have not converged; posterior summaries are unreliable.
Effective sample size (ESS) \(\text{ESS} < 400\) suggests high autocorrelation and noisy posterior estimates.

print() reports the posterior mean, SD, and the 2.5 %, 50 %, 97.5 % quantiles for the triangular shape parameters and regression coefficients, allowing inspection of the marginal posteriors and credible intervals.

9. Model comparison with LOO-CV

# LOO uses the unweighted log_lik in all models so scores are comparable
loo_baseline  <- loo(extract_log_lik(fit,          parameter_name = "log_lik"))
loo_weighted  <- loo(extract_log_lik(fit_weighted, parameter_name = "log_lik"))

print(loo_baseline)
#> 
#> Computed from 4000 by 75 log-likelihood matrix.
#> 
#>          Estimate  SE
#> elpd_loo    -31.5 2.3
#> p_loo         1.3 0.1
#> looic        63.1 4.5
#> ------
#> MCSE of elpd_loo is 0.0.
#> MCSE and ESS estimates assume independent draws (r_eff=1).
#> 
#> All Pareto k estimates are good (k < 0.7).
#> See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details.
print(loo_weighted)
#> 
#> Computed from 4000 by 75 log-likelihood matrix.
#> 
#>          Estimate  SE
#> elpd_loo    -33.6 2.2
#> p_loo         1.9 0.2
#> looic        67.2 4.5
#> ------
#> MCSE of elpd_loo is 0.0.
#> MCSE and ESS estimates assume independent draws (r_eff=1).
#> 
#> All Pareto k estimates are good (k < 0.7).
#> See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details.

loo_compare(loo_baseline, loo_weighted)
#>        elpd_diff se_diff
#> model1  0.0       0.0   
#> model2 -2.0       0.2

Leave-one-out cross-validation (LOO-CV) estimates each model’s out-of-sample predictive accuracy using Pareto-smoothed importance sampling (PSIS-LOO; Vehtari et al., 2017). The key reported quantity is:

\[\widehat{\text{elpd}}_{\text{loo}} = \sum_{n=1}^N \log p(y_n \mid \mathbf{y}_{-n})\]

where \(p(y_n \mid \mathbf{y}_{-n})\) is the leave-one-out predictive density estimated without refitting the model. Higher (less negative) values indicate better predictive accuracy.

loo_compare() ranks all three models: the top model is printed first and differences in \(\widehat{\text{elpd}}\) with standard errors are shown, so the comparison accounts for Monte Carlo uncertainty. Because log_lik is computed with the unweighted likelihood in all three Stan files, the scores are on the same scale and directly comparable.

10. Prediction helpers

# Shared helpers — work for all fitted Stan objects

tri <- function(x, a, b, c) {
  ifelse(x < a | x > b, 0,
    ifelse(x <= c,
      2 * (x - a) / ((b - a) * (c - a)),
      2 * (b - x) / ((b - a) * (b - c))))
}

predict_occurrence <- function(fit, new_x1, new_x2) {
  draws <- as.matrix(fit, pars = c("alpha", "beta1", "beta2",
                                    "a1", "c1", "b1",
                                    "a2", "c2", "b2"))
  sapply(seq_along(new_x1), function(i) {
    f1 <- tri(new_x1[i], draws[, "a1"], draws[, "b1"], draws[, "c1"])
    f2 <- tri(new_x2[i], draws[, "a2"], draws[, "b2"], draws[, "c2"])
    mean(plogis(draws[, "alpha"] + draws[, "beta1"] * f1 + draws[, "beta2"] * f2))
  })
}

predict_sd <- function(fit, new_x1, new_x2) {
  draws <- as.matrix(fit, pars = c("alpha", "beta1", "beta2",
                                    "a1", "c1", "b1",
                                    "a2", "c2", "b2"))
  sapply(seq_along(new_x1), function(i) {
    f1 <- tri(new_x1[i], draws[, "a1"], draws[, "b1"], draws[, "c1"])
    f2 <- tri(new_x2[i], draws[, "a2"], draws[, "b2"], draws[, "c2"])
    sd(plogis(draws[, "alpha"] + draws[, "beta1"] * f1 + draws[, "beta2"] * f2))
  })
}

make_pred_raster <- function(preds, template_rast, valid_idx) {
  r <- rast(template_rast)
  values(r) <- NA
  r[valid_idx] <- preds
  crop(r, ext(trim(r)))
}

# Compute predictions for all models
preds_baseline <- predict_occurrence(fit,          env_valid$temp, env_valid$prec)
preds_weighted <- predict_occurrence(fit_weighted, env_valid$temp, env_valid$prec)

sd_baseline <- predict_sd(fit,          env_valid$temp, env_valid$prec)
sd_weighted <- predict_sd(fit_weighted, env_valid$temp, env_valid$prec)

Three helpers are defined here and reused for all fitted models.

tri(x, a, b, c) — the triangular response function evaluated in R for prediction (mirrors the Stan transformed parameters block). It is vectorised over x and accepts matrices for a, b, c so it can operate on the full posterior draw matrix at once.

predict_occurrence(fit, new_x1, new_x2) — computes the posterior mean occurrence probability at each prediction location. For each location \(i\) and each posterior draw \(s = 1,\ldots,S\):

\[\hat{p}_i^{(s)} = \text{logistic}\!\left(\alpha^{(s)} + \beta_1^{(s)} f_1(x_{1i};\, a_1^{(s)}, c_1^{(s)}, b_1^{(s)}) + \beta_2^{(s)} f_2(x_{2i};\, a_2^{(s)}, c_2^{(s)}, b_2^{(s)})\right)\]

The posterior mean is then:

\[\bar{p}_i = \frac{1}{S} \sum_{s=1}^S \hat{p}_i^{(s)}\]

predict_sd(fit, new_x1, new_x2) — computes the posterior standard deviation of the occurrence probability, which serves as a spatially explicit measure of uncertainty:

\[\text{SD}_i = \sqrt{\frac{1}{S-1} \sum_{s=1}^S \left(\hat{p}_i^{(s)} - \bar{p}_i\right)^2}\]

make_pred_raster(preds, template_rast, valid_idx) — inserts the flat prediction vector back into a raster by writing values only to the non-NA cells identified by valid_idx, then crops to the non-NA extent. This reconstructs the spatial map from the vector form used during prediction.

11. Predicted occurrence maps (Step 1 — expert only)


pred_cropped_baseline <- make_pred_raster(preds_baseline, env[[1]], valid_idx)
pred_cropped_weighted <- make_pred_raster(preds_weighted, env[[1]], valid_idx)

plot(pred_cropped_baseline,
     main  = "Baseline (no confidence weighting)",
     col   = hcl.colors(100, "YlGnBu"), range = c(0, 1),
     axes  = FALSE, box = FALSE)


plot(pred_cropped_weighted,
     main  = "Option 2: Weighted likelihood model",
     col   = hcl.colors(100, "YlGnBu"), range = c(0, 1),
     axes  = FALSE, box = FALSE)

Two side-by-side occurrence probability maps are produced for the Step 1 expert-only models. Each pixel shows the posterior mean probability \(\bar{p}_i \in [0, 1]\) computed over all valid raster cells from the env stack. Comparing the three maps reveals how the confidence-handling strategy affects the spatial distribution of predicted suitability: differences are most visible in regions where low-confidence expert points are located.

12. Posterior uncertainty maps (Step 1 — expert only)

# --- Posterior SD maps ---

sd_cropped_baseline <- make_pred_raster(sd_baseline, env[[1]], valid_idx)
sd_cropped_weighted <- make_pred_raster(sd_weighted, env[[1]], valid_idx)

plot(sd_cropped_baseline,
     main = "Baseline — Uncertainty (posterior SD)",
     col  = hcl.colors(100, "Reds"), range = c(0, 0.2),axes = FALSE, box = FALSE)



plot(sd_cropped_weighted,
     main = "Option 2: Weighted — Uncertainty (posterior SD)",
     col  = hcl.colors(100, "Reds"), range = c(0, 0.2),axes = FALSE, box = FALSE)

The posterior SD maps visualise parameter uncertainty propagated through to the occurrence probability scale. High SD at a pixel indicates that the posterior draws produce a wide spread of \(\hat{p}_i^{(s)}\) values — either because the covariates at that location are near the edges of the expert-elicited range, or because the regression coefficients themselves are poorly determined. The colour scale is fixed at \([0, 0.2]\) across all three variants to allow direct visual comparison.

13. Step 2: Extracting Stan posteriors as INLA priors

INLA model - expert informed priors

# Extract mean and SD from Stan posterior to use as INLA priors.
# Switch `fit_for_inla` to `fit` (baseline) to use base model
fit_for_inla <- fit_weighted   # Option 1 ( Weighted likelihood)

stan_fixed <- summary(fit_for_inla, pars = c("alpha", "beta1", "beta2"),
                      probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975))$summary

prior_alpha_mean  <- stan_fixed["alpha",  "mean"]
prior_alpha_sd    <- stan_fixed["alpha",  "sd"]

prior_beta1_mean  <- stan_fixed["beta1",  "mean"]
prior_beta1_sd    <- stan_fixed["beta1",  "sd"]

prior_beta2_mean  <- stan_fixed["beta2",  "mean"]
prior_beta2_sd    <- stan_fixed["beta2",  "sd"]

This chunk implements the Bayesian updating step. The marginal posterior from the expert-only Stan model is summarised to a Gaussian approximation — mean and SD for each of the three fixed effects (\(\alpha\), \(\beta_1\), \(\beta_2\)) — and then used as the prior distribution for the corresponding parameter in the INLA model. The approximation is exact if the marginal posteriors are Gaussian (which NUTS typically produces for well-identified parameters).


# Convert SD to precision
prec_alpha <- 1 / prior_alpha_sd^2
prec_beta1 <- 1 / prior_beta1_sd^2
prec_beta2 <- 1 / prior_beta2_sd^2

# Build INLA-format priors from Stan posteriors
prior_alpha <- list(prior = "gaussian",
                    param = c(prior_alpha_mean, prec_alpha))

prior_beta1 <- list(prior = "gaussian",
                    param = c(prior_beta1_mean, prec_beta1))

prior_beta2 <- list(prior = "gaussian",
                    param = c(prior_beta2_mean, prec_beta2))

R-INLA parameterises Gaussian priors by their precision \(\tau = 1/\sigma^2\) rather than their standard deviation. The conversion is:

\[\tau_k = \frac{1}{\sigma_k^2}\]

where \(\sigma_k\) is the posterior SD extracted from Stan. Each prior is a named list list(prior = "gaussian", param = c(mean, precision)) in the format expected by control.fixed in inla().

14. Occurrence data preparation for INLA


monarch_occ <- monarch_sf |>
  mutate(y = monarch_sf$presenc,
         temp = terra::extract(env$temp, vect(monarch_sf))[,2],
         prec = terra::extract(env$prec, vect(monarch_sf))[,2]) |>
  select(c("y","temp","prec"))

head(monarch_occ, 5)
#> Simple feature collection with 5 features and 3 fields
#> Geometry type: POINT
#> Dimension:     XY
#> Bounding box:  xmin: -13318290 ymin: 5441616 xmax: -8161142 ymax: 6639590
#> Projected CRS: WGS 84 / Pseudo-Mercator
#>   y      temp       prec                  geometry
#> 1 0 0.8344056 -0.3107466 POINT (-12804343 6639590)
#> 2 0 1.5428125 -1.6692320 POINT (-13318290 6291657)
#> 3 1 1.7700549  1.3459833  POINT (-8588996 5441616)
#> 4 1 1.6409173  3.2275828  POINT (-8161142 5779724)
#> 5 0 1.4921930 -1.2170853 POINT (-13256622 6491688)

The monarch occurrence dataset (monarch_sf) is enriched with standardised covariate values extracted at each occurrence point from the env stack. The response variable y is taken from the presenc field. This creates the dataset used in Step 2 to update the expert-informed priors with occurrence records from biodiversity databases.


inla_df <- data.frame(
  y  = as.integer(monarch_occ$y),
  x1 = monarch_occ$temp,
  x2 = monarch_occ$prec
)

A plain data frame with columns y, x1, x2 is created for use inside the INLA stack, which requires non-spatial tabular objects.

15. SPDE mesh and spatial random effect

# Convert points to SPDE mesh for spatial autocorrelation
coords <- as.matrix(sf::st_coordinates(monarch_occ))

pedge1 <- 0.01
edge1 <- min(c(diff(st_bbox(study_area)[c(1,3)])*pedge1,diff(st_bbox(study_area)[c(2,4)])*pedge1))


mesh <- inla.mesh.2d(loc.domain = NULL,
                     max.edge = c(edge1,edge1*3),
                     min.angle = 21,
                     cutoff = edge1/1,
                     offset = c(edge1,edge1*3),
                     boundary = study_area,
                     crs = st_crs(study_area))


spde <- inla.spde2.pcmatern(
  mesh  = mesh,
  #prior.range = c(500000, 0.05),
  #prior.sigma = c(1, 0.01)
  prior.range = c(10, 0.05),
  prior.sigma = c(1, 0.05)
)
iset  <- inla.spde.make.index("s", n.spde = spde$n.spde)
A_est <- inla.spde.make.A(mesh, loc = coords)

stack_est <- inla.stack(
  data    = list(y = inla_df$y),
  A       = list(A_est, 1),
  effects = list(iset,
                 data.frame(intercept = 1,
                            x1 = inla_df$x1,
                            x2 = inla_df$x2)),
  tag     = "est"
)

formula <- y ~ -1 + intercept + x1 + x2 + f(s, model = spde)

The spatial random effect accounts for residual spatial autocorrelation in occurrence records, clustering that is not explained by temperature or precipitation.

Mesh. inla.mesh.2d() builds a constrained Delaunay triangulation over the study area. The inner maximum edge length is 1 % of the shorter bounding-box dimension (edge1); the outer buffer uses edges up to three times larger to avoid boundary effects. min.angle = 21 prevents poorly shaped triangles that degrade numerical precision.

SPDE/Matérn model. The continuous spatial field \(\xi(\mathbf{s})\) is modelled as a Gaussian process with a Matérn covariance:

\[\text{Cov}\!\left(\xi(\mathbf{s}),\, \xi(\mathbf{s}')\right) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2^{\nu-1}\,\Gamma(\nu)} \left(\frac{\|\mathbf{s}-\mathbf{s}'\|\sqrt{2\nu}}{\rho}\right)^\nu K_\nu\!\left(\frac{\|\mathbf{s}-\mathbf{s}'\|\sqrt{2\nu}}{\rho}\right)\]

where \(\rho\) is the practical range (distance at which correlation falls to approximately 0.1) and \(\sigma\) is the marginal standard deviation. The SPDE approach of Lindgren et al. (2011) represents \(\xi(\mathbf{s})\) as a Gaussian Markov Random Field on the mesh, making computation tractable via sparse matrix methods.

PC-Matérn priors. Penalised complexity (PC) priors are placed on the range and SD:

\[P(\rho < 10) = 0.05 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{penalises short ranges (spatial overfit)}\] \[P(\sigma > 1) = 0.05 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{penalises large random-effect variance}\]

Projector matrix. inla.spde.make.A() computes the \(N \times M\) sparse projector matrix \(\mathbf{A}\) that bilinearly interpolates the mesh basis functions to the observation locations (\(M\) = number of mesh nodes). The spatial field at observation \(n\) is then \(\xi(\mathbf{s}_n) = \sum_m A_{nm} u_m\).

Full model. The INLA model for observation \(n\) is:

\[y_n \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p_n)\] \[\text{logit}(p_n) = \alpha + \beta_1 x_{1n} + \beta_2 x_{2n} + \xi(\mathbf{s}_n)\]

The formula y ~ -1 + intercept + x1 + x2 + f(s, model = spde) uses an explicit intercept column (the -1 suppresses the automatic intercept) so that the control.fixed prior can be applied to it by name.

16. Prediction with INLA


coords_pred <- as.matrix(env_valid[, c("x", "y")])

# Projector matrix for prediction locations
A_pred <- inla.spde.make.A(mesh, loc = coords_pred)

stack_pred <- inla.stack(
  data    = list(y = NA),
  A       = list(A_pred, 1),
  effects = list(
    iset,
    data.frame(intercept = 1,
               x1 = env_valid$temp,
               x2 = env_valid$prec)
  ),
  tag = "pred"
)

# Join estimation + prediction stacks
stack_full <- inla.stack(stack_est, stack_pred)

# Refit on full stack
fit_inla_full <- inla(
  formula,
  family = "binomial",
  data   = inla.stack.data(stack_full),

  control.fixed = list(
    mean = list(intercept = prior_alpha_mean,
                x1        = prior_beta1_mean,
                x2        = prior_beta2_mean),
    prec = list(intercept = prec_alpha,
                x1        = prec_beta1,
                x2        = prec_beta2)
  ),

  control.predictor = list(
    A       = inla.stack.A(stack_full),
    compute = TRUE,
    link    = 1
  ),

  control.compute = list(dic = TRUE, waic = TRUE, config = TRUE)
)

# Extract predictions for pred stack only
idx_pred   <- inla.stack.index(stack_full, tag = "pred")$data

pred_mean  <- fit_inla_full$summary.fitted.values[idx_pred, "mean"]
pred_sd    <- fit_inla_full$summary.fitted.values[idx_pred, "sd"]

To generate a prediction map without refitting, a second INLA stack (stack_pred) is created over all valid raster cells. Setting y = NA for the prediction rows tells INLA to compute the posterior predictive distribution at those locations but not use them in the likelihood. The estimation and prediction stacks are then joined into stack_full and a single inla() call fits the model and simultaneously computes predictions.

control.fixed passes the Stan-derived Gaussian priors to INLA as (mean, precision) pairs for each fixed effect. With link = 1 and compute = TRUE, summary.fitted.values returns the posterior mean and SD of \(p_n\) (on the probability scale, after applying the logit link inverse) for each row in the full stack. The prediction rows are extracted by their tag index.

control.compute requests DIC and WAIC for model comparison and config = TRUE for access to the internal GMRF configuration (needed for posterior sampling if required later).

pred_raster <- rast(env$temp)
values(pred_raster) <- NA
pred_raster[valid_idx] <- pred_mean

sd_raster <- rast(env$temp)
values(sd_raster) <- NA
sd_raster[valid_idx] <- pred_sd

non_na_extent <- ext(trim(pred_raster))
pred_cropped <- crop(pred_raster, non_na_extent)
plot(pred_cropped,
     main = "Model update - Species Distribution Model",
     col  = hcl.colors(100, "YlGnBu"),
     range = c(0, 1),
     axes   = FALSE,
     box    = FALSE)



non_na_extent <- ext(trim(sd_raster))
sd_cropped <- crop(sd_raster, non_na_extent)
plot(sd_cropped,
     main = "Model update - Uncertainty (posterior SD)",
     col  = hcl.colors(100, "Reds"),
     axes   = FALSE,
     box    = FALSE)

The flat prediction and SD vectors are inserted back into rasters using the same valid_idx mask and then cropped to the non-NA extent. These maps represent the updated species distribution model: the expert knowledge from Step 1 has been used as informative priors, and the model has been updated with the larger monarch occurrence dataset. The uncertainty map integrates both fixed-effect parameter uncertainty and spatial random-effect uncertainty.

17. Non-informative priors (baseline comparison)

INLA model - non informed priors


prior.beta <- list(prec = list(default = 1/(1)^2, Intercept = 1/(20)^2),
                   mean = list(default = 0, Intercept = 0))

A weakly informative prior structure is defined for comparison: all fixed effects are centred at 0, with the intercept having a very wide prior (\(\sigma = 20\), \(\tau = 0.0025\)) and the slope coefficients having a moderately wide prior (\(\sigma = 1\), \(\tau = 1\)). This represents a near-ignorance prior with no expert knowledge incorporated and provides a baseline against which to assess the contribution of the Step 1 expert elicitation.

fit_inla_full <- inla(
  formula,
  family = "binomial",
  data   = inla.stack.data(stack_full),
  control.fixed = prior.beta,
  control.predictor = list(
    A       = inla.stack.A(stack_full),
    compute = TRUE,
    link    = 1
  ),

  control.compute = list(dic = TRUE, waic = TRUE, config = TRUE)
)

# Extract predictions for pred stack only
idx_pred   <- inla.stack.index(stack_full, tag = "pred")$data

pred_mean  <- fit_inla_full$summary.fitted.values[idx_pred, "mean"]
pred_sd    <- fit_inla_full$summary.fitted.values[idx_pred, "sd"]

The same formula and full stack are used as in the expert-informed model, but control.fixed is replaced with the non-informative prior.beta list. All other settings are identical so that differences in the output maps can be attributed solely to the choice of prior. Comparing DIC/WAIC between this fit and the expert-informed fit quantifies the predictive benefit of incorporating expert knowledge.

pred_raster <- rast(env$temp)
values(pred_raster) <- NA
pred_raster[valid_idx] <- pred_mean

sd_raster <- rast(env$temp)
values(sd_raster) <- NA
sd_raster[valid_idx] <- pred_sd

non_na_extent <- ext(trim(pred_raster))
pred_cropped <- crop(pred_raster, non_na_extent)
plot(pred_cropped,
     main = "Non informative - Species Distribution Model",
     col  = hcl.colors(100, "YlGnBu"),
     range = c(0, 1),
     axes   = FALSE,
     box    = FALSE)



non_na_extent <- ext(trim(sd_raster))
sd_cropped <- crop(sd_raster, non_na_extent)
plot(sd_cropped,
     main = "Non informative - Uncertainty (posterior SD)",
     col  = hcl.colors(100, "Reds"),
     axes   = FALSE,
     box    = FALSE)

Occurrence probability and uncertainty maps for the non-informative INLA model, produced identically to the expert-informed maps. Visual comparison between these and the Plot-INLA maps shows how the expert priors shift the posterior mean and reduce uncertainty, particularly in data-sparse regions where the likelihood is uninformative.
